speaker: interviewer
text: With Fred Kort. What rank were you in in the army-- in the Polish Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was demobilized, I was discharged the rank of corporal-- a very low rank, but just fine for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like to come to Warsaw and to walk in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, you know, we were stationed on the east side of the Vistula River near Warszawa Praga. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were waiting-- we were anxious to-- it was a very, very cold winter, incidentally, and the Vistula was frozen solid. We could have easily walked across. But we didn't understand why we were stationed there. We didn't move. We had no order to cross the river to take in Warsaw while the Germans were battling the partisans on the other side. Afterwards, it was explained to us the people that rose up against the Nazis, in fact, was a group of Poles that took their orders from the exiled government [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the Polish government from 1939 that escaped to London. And then, subsequently, we realized that one was our-- our army. We were-- we took our orders from the Russians, which, in fact, the Armia Ludowa, the People's Army, while the others were-- were the-- the-- from under the direction of General Anders from the-- the Polish Army in exile in London. So the Russians felt let them killed himself. Then we walk in. In fact, that's exactly what happened. The Germans and the-- the-- the-- the group that rose up, they-- they killed each other. And then we moved in. But that already-- that was-- in-- and we moved in, I believe, it was late January, early February, of 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1945. And then after that, I was stationed in various barracks in various divisional areas in Poland such as Inowroclaw, Bydgoszcz, Torun, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and finally and I was discharged in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't answer my question. What was it like to go to Warsaw? Did you go to the ghetto? Did you look for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, it was basically of no interest to me. I was in the Polish Army. And I really wanted to, for me-- as a soldiers, it's always dangerous. You know, your life is always at stake. But for me, I viewed it as-- as almost like-- like a picnic, because coming out from a concentration camp where you were no-- quote, unquote, 'no human being.' Instead, you are soldier. You are protected. You got comrades. You got people. And you were respected. You were looked up to. You have power. You have a gun in your hand. Now, I-- I-- we may have gone somehow. But Warsaw was not recognizable anymore. Warsaw was terribly destroyed. Whatever-- what's left from 1939, and subsequently, a little  bit rebuilt, when the Germans and-- and the group that rose up against the Nazis, the-- the partisans, and subsequently we-- when we-- there was very, very little left of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, no Jews were left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then there was was only-- there was a song, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I remember. That's a song. You know, and-- and the remnants Warsaw, the soldiers-- in fact, we used to sing that. And to me, it was wonderful, victorious, a free person. And I wanted to forget the-- the years of-- from the camps and stuff like that. In fact, I believe many people that you may have interviewed over the years will tell you that for years and years, including yourself, we didn't want-- we didn't even want to think about it then.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's only years later, in the last 10, 15 years, where we realized we are not getting any younger. There's a story to be told. You know what? For me even, as much as I experienced it myself, it seemed so unbelievable. And over the years, periodically, I get sort of caught in relating my story. In the early years, I started, and then I stopped, because what we have to say sounds so unbelievable. We don't want to be accused of being liars.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we didn't want to know about it. We were busy earning a living, raising families and so on. But only now in our-- the evening of our lives, we feel it is important to record it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you, apropos some of the things you just said, how can we-- how can we, for example, find your name somehow in Treblinka papers? Can you prove that you were there? Do they have lists like they do in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Proof, I never felt I had to prove anything, because it is a-- it's documented and a foregone conclusion. But in fact, there is proof in more than one way. There were a  total of nine survivors of which at least seven are still alive. And I'm continuously in touch with them-- some in Israel, some in-- in-- in-- there's one in Miami that know me from there. There is one in Paris that know me from there. We get together periodically, allow me. And in 1980, when there was a world gathering of Holocaust survivors in Israel, I took it up on myself and through research, and letters, and communications, I organized, sponsored, and hosted a gathering within the gathering of the Holocaust survivors. And I was able to round up from throughout the world each and every one then nine Treblinka survivors. And I hosted a big party only for those survivors at the Tel Aviv Hilton at the King Solomon's Terrace Restaurant. And it was recorded by the publications. And we've got one right now, the And we get together periodically, we Treblinka survivors. And there is plenty of material on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a published list of people who were killed in Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No published list-- only the list that we survivors compiled ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Treblinka unlike than any other-- than most of the other camps, I should say, was not liberated. Treblinka was liquidated by the Nazis. For example, like Auschwitz was liberated. And Dachau was liberated. Majdanek was liberated. Treblinka was not liberated. We few survivors liberated ourselves. So Treblinka as such where they are close or maybe a few more than a million Jews perished. Nine survivors-- the odds are incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you mention some of the names of the other survivors and where they lived or have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. There is one. I-- incidentally, I'm probably one of the youngest survivors. There's one in Miami. His name is set Simon Friedman. He lives in Miami. There is one-- Shimon Ciegal. He lives in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ciegal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ciegal. Shimon Ciegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-B-C, Ciegal, C-I-E-G-A-L. There is a Yaja Vigovi. Her name was Yaja [? Morel ?] then. But she married. And she lives in Haifa. There is a Sigmund Broadhandle. He was in Treblinka. He escaped from-- he was-- he escaped even before the last. He escaped when he was working outside the camp about a half a year or so before they liquidated the camp. And there is-- there is-- was a Avrum Katz, who since then died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. And there is-- let me see, then there is a Benjamin Rock, an old man, very old. I saw him the last time, oh, maybe during that gathering. I understand he passed away since. There is, let me see here. I've got them all pictured. And the-- oh, who else is there? I've got to look up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we've-- we've got plenty. So these--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is recorded.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have recorded these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's also recorded in the Yad Vashem. And it's recorded in-- in the Holocaust Museum in Washington. It's recorded-- I have my own lengthy video that I related my whole life story to my children of 11 hours. And incidentally, you know, I'm very much involved. And then-- I also-- you know, I-- last year, I was appointed ambassador to the Tel Aviv foundation. And they are totally familiar with my media activities in that. And also there is a monument-- a-- a monument in-- in the Spiegel Park near the Tel Aviv Hilton where I am-- where my bronze figure there is cast for life, I hope. [LAUGHS] And by the way, you are or may not be aware, I am one of the founders of the Holocaust Museum in Washington-- one of the original founders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I saw you at the opening of the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. And I'm also-- what's called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Fred, I just want--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and-- and to verify also that, which has been documented, and-- and the most important thing that to-- to-- will be testimony to the various war crimes trials where I was witness in a variety of cities, including Chicago, Dusseldorf, in Vienna, in Stuttgart, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plus others where I wasn't witness, but I had to give testimony in writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What I wanted to ask you is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And most important--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here-- there is here in LA at the Holocaust Monument, you will see inscribed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- The Fred Kort Family Foundation as sponsors and founder on one of the pillars, which is dedicated to the fallen brethren from Treblinka. Then we have a wall, which is dedicated to my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you about the destruction of Treblinka. And-- so the Germans, at the end, blew it up. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they blew it up. I revisited first Treblinka about four weeks after I-- after I was finally liberated when I got away, out from the forest and so on. And what I saw then was just the slabs of the many barracks that were there, which would indicate to me they didn't do away with it. I think they only set matches to the barracks, because the foundations are all there. In fact, that pump where we had to get the water from is still there, but gigantic trees, because-- Bob and I, we went there about five years ago. And I got a videotape on it. And I gave some testimony in that video on my experience and of my arrival in Treblinka. I got that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you own that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I own that. If you want to splice that in here, I'd be glad too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, what happened to you? Can you give us a little overview of what happened in the next 50 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then finally, when I was discharged from the army. And I somehow left Poland through Czechoslovakia, and I wound up in Vienna there-- in a little refugee camp there for a few weeks. And from there, I went to Salzburg. And then finally, I left that group and made myself and my way back to my little village in Germany. And now comes a very important thing. And I came back to my village [INAUDIBLE] the time that I mentioned before. And I worked there for the American military government in the De-Nazification Department, which in-- in the provincial capital was an half an hour by bus from every morning I went there. One day, I came back from work. The burgermeister the mayor of that little town, summoned me to his office. He said, I just got the telegram from your mother. She sent the telegram and said, this is Yedi Kort. I'm here with my daughter, Edith, which were just-- we here in the refugee camp in Neumunster near Hamburg. We just returned from Russia. We were interned there during the entire war until July 1946. The Russians called us [RUSSIAN]. And [? they ?] were people that they didn't consider trustworthy because they spoke-- the only language they spoke was German. And since they were at war with Germans, they thought they were really bad. They interned there in-- in-- near the Ural Mountains. Anyway, so what a reunion that was. And I went up there. And I picked them up and brought them back to my village after six and a half years. Subsequently, I already-- before then, I already had made documents from my immigration to the US. And my mother insisted I should go through with it. So that was mid '46. And in January, I left Germany on my way to-- to come to the US. And I intended to bring my sister, followed me five years later. Unfortunately, one year after I left, my mother passed away. So I could not bring her. My sister, incidentally, she also perished. She didn't perish. She died about 13, 14 years ago. And so I'm really the only survivor from that immediate family of about 50 some odd people, except I have one cousin that somehow survived in Denmark. She lives in-- in Copenhagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother perished in Lodz. I believe I have not mentioned that. But I skipped Lodz. And subsequent, I heard-- or did I mention it-- that he died, I believe, in 1943 or 1944 in the Lodz ghetto before they liquidated and took the remaining inhabitants to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came here and you married and had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then I-- I came to the US in March of 1947. And I first landed in New York. I-- I got a job as a factory worker. And then people told me go west. I went west. I went to Massachusetts. And it was western Massachusetts. And I worked for General Electric there. Then I asked for a transfer to California, which I did. And after about four months, I left them. And I got a good position as the assistant hotel electrician at the Biltmore Hotel downtown here in Los Angeles. During my working period at the Biltmore, I met a gentleman in the hotel that came here from the east to set up a toy factory. And he had some problems with his desk lamp. And I was asked to go up and fix it. And I fixed his desk clamp. And he asked me if I knew somebody that's handy. And he needed somebody to help him set up a factory. And I said, yes, I knew someone. That's me. And that was in May of 1949. I joined him to set up that factory. And ever since then, I am in the toy business.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right now, fortunately, I'm proud to say I'm one of the leading toy companies in the industry. And in terms of units of toys, while I'm not the largest toy company, but because of the price points, which are more popularly priced, in terms units, I'm probably the largest toy factory in the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we have-- I have factories in Los Angeles, in Tijuana, in Toronto, and the Far East. We have distribution centers in-- in-- in Memphis, Tennessee, in offices in New York, and in Hong Kong, and Toronto, and Los Angeles, Budapest, and Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you decided to make low price toys so that more children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's just maybe coincidentally, low price toys most naturally are more affordable with most of the-- the-- the, oh, mainstream American population can afford it. So I'm in that. And I would like to say it right now I am the proud father of four children. And I have a wonderful wife. It's the second time around. But I'm married to her already for 22 years. So I've got one child with her, a little girl. And I have three wonderful sons that work with me in my company and very astute and very hard working. And I am very proud of them.

words.text: I am very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names-- the oldest is Jordan, then comes Steven, then comes David. My little girl's name-- little? She is now 15 going on 16. Her name is Suzy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the proud grandfather of six wonderful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrific.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my sons are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could speak to people in-- 200 years from now, your great-great grandchildren, what would you like to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I could come back for just a short period in 200 years from now, and incidentally, I intend to come back in 200 years just for a short little while to see how things are. At the time, I'm going to be a butterfly then. But that-- that's OK. But I still will be able to talk. I would tell them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to really make it a point and familiarize themselves what happened 200 years earlier, to the world, and the miscellaneous nations of the world, and in particular, the Jews that suffered and how proud all of them came-- became after the establishment of the Jewish nation, Israel. And I would say that's probably the most wonderful thing that happened to the Jews of the world because as long as there's an Israel, every Jew whether they lives in Argentina, in the Soviet Union, in Ethiopia, South America, or the Far East-- wherever they live-- or the United States, they are looked up to with respect that unfortunately, we did not have when we did not have a homeland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We couldn't go anywhere, could we then? But now there is an Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, thank you very much. We could go on for the whole night and talk about this more and more.

words.text: talk about this more and more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Especially, your chutzpah stories of which there are many more than we got down on videotape. And now, I'd like to show-- I'd like you to show your photographs. I know you have some very special ones. So we're going to do that now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
